species.mnemonic: Cheerily, cheer-up, cheerily — a caroling whistle at dawn and dusk

sound_types.song: Rich caroling whistle, phrases of 2-3 syllables with rising and falling pitch
sound_types.call: Sharp tut-tut-tut alarm; also a thin, high seep in flight

species.confuser_notes: Varied Thrush has a single eerie buzzy note on one pitch, not a caroling phrase

candidates.location: Dr. R. J. Allan Hogg Rotary Park (near  White Rock), Metro Vancouver, British Columbia
candidates.remarks:
A few female House Finches had assembled near a bird bath, on some kale plants which have several dried seed pods. These birds have been feeding on these seed pods for the past few days. One of the birds was making this call starting at 00:05.

Filtering (in sequence): High Pass 200 Hz @ 12 dB. Normalization @ -10dB. High Pass 1200 Hz @ 6 dB. Fade in / fade out.

I have the unaltered file if needed as well.

candidates.location: Mx, Distrito Federal, Lomas de Bezares, Calle Colina
candidates.remarks: wildtronics

candidates.location: Lafayette, Boulder County, Colorado
candidates.remarks: A House Finch belting out a tune from a tall cottonwood. The bird did a long warmup of varied calls before it started singing. Sunny and cold when I made this recording.

candidates.location: Dr. R. J. Allan Hogg Rotary Park (near  White Rock), Metro Vancouver, British Columbia
candidates.remarks:
One female House Finch was drinking out of a bird bath. A second female House Finch approached it from behind, and displaced it from the bird bath. They were both hovering in the air, facing each other, wings flapping rapidly, after which the first bird flew off and the second took its place. The action unfolded rapidly. The recorder was placed near the bird bath.

Filtering (in sequence)
- High Pass 200 Hz @ 12 dB
- Normalization @ -10dB
- High Pass 1200 Hz @ 6 dB
- Fade in / fade out

I have the unaltered file if needed as well.

Cypress Point, Sardis Lower Lake. Open hardwood grove along lakeshore.

Fostex FR-2 digital field recorder with Telinga Pro 22" parabolic reflector

candidates.location: Big Bear, San Bernardino County, California
candidates.remarks: Residential area of pines and junipers, bird came down to a dripping water faucet, took some sips, fluttered wings as if begging or bathing (but not sufficient water) while making this begging-like call, then flew to lowest branch of juniper tree and continued this call while fluttering wings in a begging behavior. Unfortunately I'm not sure whether it was an adult or immature. It appeared to be an adult and it would be unlikely to have a near-adult-plumage bird in May, but the field guide says juvenile are possible May and after and I didn't get on the key field marks of the yellow bill base of a juvenile or the subtle thin blue eyebrow of an adult pacific.

candidates.location: West Sacramento, Yolo County, California
candidates.remarks:
I modified the recording with Adobe Audition by shortening it so that I only preserved the territorial sound of the Anna’s Hummingbird’s tail chirp. I lowered background noise too, especially below 1k. (It was a windy day.)
This hummingbird was flying diving between hedgerows south of the Stonegate Pond and east of the Clarksburg Trail. 
The hummingbird flew 10 meters or so, then shot to the bottom of his arc where his tail feathers created the high-pitched, high-volume chirp. I don’t know if he did it because I was there or he was courting a female or defending his turf.

candidates.location: Cape Flattery, Neah Bay, Clallam County, Washington
candidates.remarks: Point-blank calls... I was searching for a rare bird, and had ducked into a large bush. While in the bush, this Chestnut-backed Chickadee landed about 2 feet to the left of my ear, and I froze and quietly hit "record" on my Olympus, which I'd had in hand the whole time. Habitat: tall evergreens with low brush on high, rocky cliffs above the ocean. Migrant trap. Weather: mild, sunny. HPF used.

candidates.location: Swan Lake, Victoria, Vancouver Island, BC
candidates.remarks:
Calls and songs from several individuals feeding on ground and close to ground in bushes in flooded wetlands.

This recording was made on the northern edge of Swan Lake, two days after heavy rains flooded the lake's surrounding grasslands.  Main trails were still flooded and many bird species moved out of lake areas into surrounding flooded grasslands and forests.  At least six Virginia Rails were observed in one hour in the flooded areas, which is a larger number that usually seen.

confuser_pairs.label: Black-capped vs Chestnut-backed Chickadee
confuser_pairs.key_difference: Black-capped: slower dee-dee-dee + clear fee-bee song. Chestnut-backed: faster, higher, no fee-bee.

confuser_pairs.label: Song Sparrow vs Golden-crowned Sparrow
confuser_pairs.key_difference: Song Sparrow: 3 notes + complex trill. Golden-crowned: 3 pure descending whistles, no trill.

confuser_pairs.label: American Crow vs Steller's Jay
confuser_pairs.key_difference: Crow: clean caw-caw. Jay: harsh shack-shack, more grating and varied.

confuser_pairs.label: Dark-eyed Junco vs Spotted Towhee
confuser_pairs.key_difference: Junco: even trill on one pitch. Towhee: 2 intro notes THEN a trill.

confuser_pairs.label: House Finch vs Song Sparrow
confuser_pairs.key_difference: House Finch: continuous warble ending in buzz. Song Sparrow: structured 3-note opening.

lesson_plan.description: 5 lessons of 3 birds each, ordered by distinctiveness (most distinctive first)

lessons.title: The unmistakable three
lessons.rationale: Loud, distinctive, impossible to confuse with each other. Builds confidence.

lessons.title: Backyard singers
lessons.rationale: Classic songs you'll hear every morning. Introduces the fee-bee mnemonic.

lessons.title: The sparrow start
lessons.rationale: First confuser challenge — three sparrow-like species with distinct songs.

lessons.title: Small and buzzy
lessons.rationale: Complex songs that reward repeated listening. Wren vs finch structure.

lessons.title: Flock sounds and mimics
lessons.rationale: Contact calls and mimicry. Introduces the chickadee confuser pair.
